1: אֵלֶּה תּוֹלְדֹת (ʾēlleh tôlĕḏōt) — "these are the generations/account of." The tôlĕḏōt formula (cf. 2:4; 5:1; 6:9; 11:10; 11:27; 25:12, 19; 36:1; 37:2) opens a new section of Genesis. Here it introduces the Table of Nations — not a genealogy in the strict biological sense but a political-ethnic mapping of the known world from Noah's three sons. The organizing principle is threefold: clans (mišpĕḥōtām), languages (bilšōnōtām), and lands/nations (bĕʾarṣōtām lĕgôyêhem) — the same formula closes each of the three son-sections (vv. 5, 20, 31) and the chapter itself (v. 32).
2: Japheth's seven sons are broadly associated with the northern and western peoples of the ancient Near East: גֹּמֶר (Gomer) = Cimmerians (Gimirrai in Assyrian); מָגוֹג (Māgōg) = a northern people, associated with eschatological enemies in Ezek 38-39; מָדַי (Māḏay) = Medes; יָוָן (Yāwān) = Ionia/Greeks (Yavan in Sanskrit and cognate languages); תֻּבָל (Tûbāl) = Tabal (Anatolia); מֶשֶׁךְ (Mešeḵ) = Mushki (Cappadocia); תִּירָס (Tîrās) = Tyrrhenians/Thracians (identification uncertain). The list is not intended to be exhaustive but representative of the Japhethite sphere.
3: Gomer's sons: אַשְׁכְּנַז (ʾAšḵĕnaz) = Scythians (Aškuzai in Assyrian sources); רִיפַת (Rîpaṯ) = possibly Paphlagonians or Celts; תֹּגַרְמָה (Tôḡarmāh) = Til-garimmu, a region in southeastern Anatolia, associated with horse-trading (Ezek 27:14; 38:6). These second-generation descendants of Japheth begin to fill in the northwestern tier of the ancient world known to the author.
4: Javan's sons represent the Greek world and its periphery: אֱלִישָׁה (ʾĔlîšāh) = Alashiya (Cyprus) or possibly Elis; תַּרְשִׁישׁ (Taršîš) = Tartessos in Spain or possibly Sardinia — a far-western trading destination (cf. Jonah 1:3; 1 Kgs 10:22); כִּתִּים (Kittîm) = Kition in Cyprus, later used broadly for western maritime powers including Rome (Dan 11:30; Num 24:24); דֹּדָנִים (Dōḏānîm) = possibly Dodaneans/Rhodians, or (with textual variant) Rodanites.
5: מֵאֵלֶּה נִפְרְדוּ אִיֵּי הַגּוֹיִם — "from these the coastland peoples of the nations were separated/spread out." Nāpraḏ (Niphal of pāraḏ, to be separated, divided) — the same verb root as Peleg's name (10:25) and implicit in the Babel scattering. The coastland peoples (ʾiyyê haggôyim = islands/coastlands of the nations) identifies the Japhethites as the maritime-western peoples. The threefold formula closes this section: lĕmišpĕḥōtām bĕlišōnōtām bĕʾarṣōtām bĕgôyêhem — by their clans, by their languages, in their lands, within their nations.
6: Ham's sons map onto Africa and the ancient Near East: כּוּשׁ (Kûš) = Ethiopia/Nubia; מִצְרַיִם (Miṣrāyim) = Egypt (the dual form suggests Upper and Lower Egypt); פּוּט (Pûṭ) = Libya or Somalia (identification debated; Nah 3:9 places Put near Egypt); כְּנָעַן (Kĕnaʿan) = Canaan. The listing of Canaan last, after three African/Nilotic peoples, places Canaan in a subordinate position — and given the curse of 9:25, this positioning is significant. Ham's line will include both the great imperial powers (Egypt, Babylon through Nimrod) and the promised-land occupants.
7: Cush's sons extend southward and eastward: שְׁבָא (Šĕbāʾ) = Saba/Sheba (Arabia or East Africa — the Queen of Sheba, 1 Kgs 10:1); דְּדָן (Dĕḏān) = an Arabian trading center (Jer 25:23; Ezek 27:15-20). These Cushite-descended peoples in Arabia represent the commercial network of the ancient incense trade routes.
8: וְנִמְרֹד הֵחֵל לִהְיוֹת גִּבֹּר בָּאָרֶץ — "Nimrod began to be a mighty warrior/hero in the earth." Gîbbōr (mighty warrior, hero — from gābar, to be strong, prevail) is used of warriors (2 Sam 23:8-39), Saul and Jonathan (2 Sam 1:19, 25, 27), and God himself as the divine warrior (Ps 24:8; Isa 42:13; Zeph 3:17). The Hiphil hēḥēl (began) echoes 4:26 (people began to call on the LORD's name) and 6:1 (people began to multiply) — the verb marks significant new beginnings, not always positive. Nimrod is the first named human "mighty one" — a political-military category that sets him apart from the genealogical norm.
9: גִּבֹּר-צַיִד לִפְנֵי יְהוָה (gibbōr-ṣayiḏ lipnê YHWH) — "a mighty hunter before the LORD." The expression lipnê YHWH (before the LORD, in YHWH's sight) can mean: (1) in God's presence/sight (a positive superlative — supremely great); (2) before/in front of God (as a rival or opponent — the Targum Onqelos adds "in defiance of"). The proverb attached ("like Nimrod a mighty hunter before the LORD") preserves an ancient saying. Given Nimrod's connection to Babel (v. 10), the "defiance" reading has textual support — the great-city builder and the one who hunted before God fit the Babel narrative's spirit of rival-to-divine ambition.
10: רֵאשִׁית מַמְלַכְתּוֹ בָּבֶל (rēʾšît mamlaktô bābel) — "the beginning of his kingdom was Babylon." Bābel appears here first in Genesis — immediately before the Babel narrative of ch. 11. Nimrod's kingdom (the first named human kingdom in the OT) begins at Babel and extends to Nineveh — the two great enemy cities of the OT prophets. שִׁנְעָר (Šinʿār) = Sumer/Babylonia — the same region as the Babel event (11:2). Nimrod's Babel is the historical/political root of the Babel tower-city project.
11: מִן-הָאָרֶץ הַהִוא יָצָא אַשּׁוּר — either "from that land he [Nimrod] went to Assyria" or "from that land Asshur went out" (subject ambiguous). If Nimrod, he extends his empire northward to Assyria — consistent with a Hamite building the great Assyrian cities. If Asshur, the text may simply trace the geographical spread of Assyria. The cities named — נִינְוֵה (Nînĕwēh, Nineveh), the great Assyrian capital — are historically attested from at least the 3rd millennium BCE.
12: הִוא הָעִיר הַגְּדֹלָה — "that is the great city." The designation "great city" (hāʿîr haggĕḏōlāh) is used in Jonah 1:2; 3:2-3; 4:11 for Nineveh — the city of 120,000 people that Jonah was sent to preach in. This parenthetical label connects the Table of Nations genealogy to the Jonah narrative — Nineveh's greatness originates in Nimrod's founding, and its significance will appear in the prophetic literature of 2 Kings and Jonah.
13: מִצְרַיִם (Miṣrāyim, Egypt) as father of peoples — the sons of Egypt are the peoples inhabiting the Nile Delta and surrounding regions. לוּדִים (Lûḏîm) = Lydians or Libyans; פְּלִשְׁתִּים (Pĕlištîm, Philistines) are traced in v. 14 to the Kasluhites through Egypt — placing Israel's great adversaries within the Hamitic genealogical framework. The Philistines originating from Egyptian stock is consistent with Egyptian administrative presence in Canaan during the Late Bronze Age.
14: כַּסְלֻחִים אֲשֶׁר יָצְאוּ מִשָּׁם פְּלִשְׁתִּים — "the Kasluhites, from whom the Philistines came." The parenthetical identification of the Philistines' Egyptian origin is textually noted but geographically the Philistines are most closely associated with the Aegean Sea Peoples (the Peleset of Egyptian records, 12th century BCE) who settled in Canaan's coastal plain. כַּפְתֹּרִים (Kaptōrîm) = Cretans — Amos 9:7 explicitly identifies the Philistines' origin as Caphtor (Crete), making the Kasluhite parenthetical and the Caphtorite line different traditional strands.
15: כְּנַעַן יָלַד אֶת-צִידֹן בְּכֹרוֹ — "Canaan fathered Sidon his firstborn." Ṣîḏōn (Sidon) is named the firstborn son of Canaan — the great Phoenician coastal city, Israel's northern neighbor and the source of the cedars of Lebanon. The list that follows (Hittites, Jebusites, Amorites, Girgashites, Hivites, etc.) is the standard Canaanite nations list of the Pentateuch (cf. Exod 3:8, 17; 23:23; 33:2; Deut 7:1; 20:17) — the Table of Nations is also a land-grant document, mapping the territory that Israel will later claim as Abrahamic promise fulfilled.
16: יְבוּסִי (Yĕbûsî, Jebusites) — the pre-Israelite inhabitants of Jerusalem (2 Sam 5:6-9; Josh 15:63). אֱמֹרִי (ʾĔmōrî, Amorites) — a broad term for highland Canaanites (cf. Num 13:29; Josh 10:5-6; Amos 2:9-10). These peoples are genealogically located as Canaanites descended from Ham — the Abrahamic covenant will involve displacing Ham's cursed line (9:25-27) in fulfillment of the blessing on Shem.
17: חִוִּי (Ḥiwwî, Hivites) — inhabitants of Gibeon (Josh 9:7; 11:19) and Shechem (Gen 34:2). עַרְקִי (ʿArqî, Arkites) — from Tell Arqa in northern Lebanon. סִינִי (Sînî, Sinites) — possibly from the region around Tell Kazel in northern Lebanon (the Siyannu of Ugaritic texts). These third-tier names in the Canaanite list represent specific city-states and clans rather than major ethnic groups.
18: וְאַחַר נָפֹצוּ מִשְׁפְּחוֹת הַכְּנַעֲנִי — "afterward the clans of the Canaanites spread abroad." Nāpōṣ (Niphal of pûṣ, to scatter, be dispersed) — the same verb as the Babel scattering (11:4: pen-nāpûṣ) and its fulfillment (11:8-9). The Canaanite dispersion anticipates the Babel narrative's scattering and the later displacement of Canaan by Israel as fulfillment of 9:25-27.
19: The geographical boundaries of Canaan are given: from Sidon southward to Gaza (Mediterranean coast), then inland toward Sodom, Gomorrah, Admah, Zeboiim to Lasha. The southern boundary cities (Sodom, Gomorrah, Admah, Zeboiim) are exactly the cities of the plain that will be destroyed in ch. 19 — the Table of Nations implicitly marks the doomed cities within the Canaanite territorial definition. Canaan's eastern boundary reaches toward the Dead Sea region.
20: The closing formula for Ham's descendants: ʾēlleh bĕnê-ḥām lĕmišpĕḥōtām bilšōnōtām bĕʾarṣōtām bĕgôyêhem — "these are the sons of Ham by their clans, by their languages, in their lands, in their nations." The fourfold formula (clans / languages / lands / nations) is identical for all three sons (vv. 5, 20, 31) — the same structural grid organizes all three genealogical lists, suggesting intentional literary organization of the total human family.
21: וּלְשֵׁם יֻלַּד גַּם-הוּא — "and to Shem also children were born." The narrative interest tips toward Shem at this point — he is identified as ʾăbî kol-bĕnê-ʿēber (ancestor of all the sons of Eber). עֵבֶר (ʿĒber) — the eponymous ancestor of the Hebrews (ʿibrî). The same consonants spell "crossing over" (ʿābēr) — possibly signifying those who crossed the Euphrates, those from "the other side" (Josh 24:2-3). Shem's genealogical line will narrow through Eber to Peleg to Terah to Abram — the Table of Nations is also a road map to the covenant line.
22: Shem's five sons: עֵילָם (ʿÊlām) = Elam (southwestern Iran); אַשּׁוּר (ʾAššûr) = Assyria; אַרְפַּכְשַׁד (ʾArpaḵšaḏ) = uncertain identification (possibly the Chaldean homeland or Arrapkha in northern Iraq) — the ancestor of Eber and thus of Abram; לוּד (Lûḏ) = Lydia (western Anatolia); אֲרָם (ʾĂrām) = Aram/Syria. Through Arphaxad runs the Abrahamic covenant line.
23: Aram's sons represent the Aramaic-speaking peoples: עוּץ (ʿÛṣ) = the land of Uz, home of Job (Job 1:1; Lam 4:21); חוּל (Ḥûl), גֶּתֶר (Geter), מַשׁ (Maš or Meshech, variant reading) — these represent the Aramaean city-states that will be significant in Israel's political history (Syria, 2 Sam 8:6; 1 Kgs 20:34; 2 Kgs 16:5-9).
24: וְאַרְפַּכְשַׁד יָלַד אֶת-שָׁלַח וְשֶׁלַח יָלַד אֶת-עֵבֶר — "Arphaxad fathered Shelah, and Shelah fathered Eber." The genealogical line is narrowing: from Shem's five sons, through Arphaxad alone, to Eber. This line will continue in the post-Babel genealogy of ch. 11 with even greater specificity, leading to Terah and Abram. The Table of Nations is simultaneously a full-world ethnography and a progressive narrowing toward the covenant recipient.
25: וּלְעֵבֶר יֻלַּד שְׁנֵי בָנִים שֵׁם הָאֶחָד פֶּלֶג כִּי בְיָמָיו נִפְלְגָה הָאָרֶץ — "Two sons were born to Eber: the name of the one was Peleg, for in his days the earth was divided." Peleḡ from pālaḡ (to divide, split) — the wordplay is explicit. "In his days the earth was divided" — most naturally referring to the Babel event of ch. 11, where God divides/scatters humanity. Peleg's name thus timestamps the Babel dispersion: it happened within his generation. Alternatively, some read "divided" as a reference to continental geography (less likely given the context). The genealogical note connects the Table of Nations to the Babel narrative chronologically.
26: Joktan's thirteen sons (vv. 26-29) represent the various tribes and regions of southern Arabia — Almodad, Sheleph, Hazarmaveth (= Hadramawt in Yemen), Jerah (lunar-deity name), and others. These are among the most obscure names in the Table of Nations; several can be tentatively connected to known South Arabian place names and peoples through archaeology and comparative Semitic studies.
27: Hadoram, Uzal, Diklah — these Joktanite names continue the South Arabian tribal geography. עוּזָל (ʿûzāl) may correspond to the ancient city Uzal (= Sanaa in Yemen, according to Arabic tradition). The density of names in the Joktanite list without additional narrative material signals that these peoples were known to the author's audience but peripheral to the biblical narrative's primary interests.
28: Obal, Abimael, Sheba — שְׁבָא (Šĕbāʾ) appears both as a Cushite son (v. 7, through Ham) and here as a Joktanite son (through Shem). The double occurrence may reflect the two known Sheba/Saba regions — one East African, one South Arabian — or the fact that the same tribal name existed in two different genealogical traditions. The Queen of Sheba (1 Kgs 10:1-13) is associated with the South Arabian Saba.
29: אוֹפִר (ʾÔpîr, Ophir) — the famous source of gold sought by Solomon's navy (1 Kgs 9:28; 10:11; 22:48; Job 22:24; 28:16; Ps 45:9; Isa 13:12). Ophir's precise location is debated (India, Arabia, or East Africa), but its genealogical placement among the Joktanite South Arabians tips toward an Arabian or East African identification. יוֹבָב (Yôbāb) — possibly connected etymologically to the name Job (ʾiyyôb), though the identification is uncertain.
30: The geographical extent of Joktan's descendants — from Mesha to Sephar in the eastern hill country — is another pair of location-markers that resist confident modern identification. Meša and Sĕpar likely represent the western and eastern limits of the South Arabian region where the Joktanite tribes settled.
31: The Shem-section closing formula mirrors v. 5 and v. 20: lĕmišpĕḥōtām bilšōnōtām bĕʾarṣōtām lĕgôyêhem — "by their clans, by their languages, in their lands, by their nations." The Semitic peoples, including the ancestors of both the Arabians (through Joktan) and the Hebrews (through Peleg to Eber to Arphaxad to Shem), are mapped out before the Babel narrative that explains why they are now distributed.
32: מֵאֵלֶּה נִפְרְדוּ הַגּוֹיִם בָּאָרֶץ אַחַר הַמַּבּוּל — "from these the nations were separated/scattered in the earth after the flood." The verb nāpraḏ (Niphal of pāraḏ, to separate, be scattered) provides the theological bracket: the diversity of nations was not an accident but a separation. The Table of Nations implicitly anticipates the Babel narrative's explanation of how this separation happened. The phrase "after the flood" grounds the entire human diversification in the post-Noah world — the whole table is a post-diluvian re-population.
